reason: The item cleanly tests stage-dependent mechanism reasoning: intervention at the trigger stage (negative electrode) versus heat dominance during full runaway (positive electrode), with a clear boundary at SEI-decomposition onset. Decoys are mostly single-fault and plausibly tempting by exploiting common expert pitfalls (stage conflation, boundary extension, and causal overattribution), yielding good discrimination. Minor neutrality and ambiguity risks arise from absolute wording (“does not affect,” “reliably”) and from requiring solvers to reconcile a multi-select format where one correct option (A) is counterintuitive and could be perceived as overly strong without careful reading.

The item cleanly tests boundary-aware admissibility around SEI growth vs SEI decomposition and thermal-runaway initiation, and the two correct options are strongly separable from common overgeneralizations. However, several decoys rely on broadly similar extrapolation/overreach patterns, which modestly reduces discriminative sharpness and single-fault purity even though they remain engineering-plausible.

The item cleanly targets stage-dependent causal intervention logic: suppressing the initiating negative-electrode-driven cascade pre-SEI decomposition versus attempting to cool a dominant heat source after runaway is fully developed. It has strong discriminative value against the common “largest heat source is best target” heuristic, though as a short-answer prompt it leaves some ambiguity about what “onset of full thermal runaway” operationally means, which slightly weakens boundary tightness and decoy-style single-fault control.
